examples.title: Thread Creation and Join
examples.description: Basic demonstration of creating threads and waiting for their completion
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates pthread creation using pthread_create() and waiting for completion using pthread_join(). Each thread executes the threadFunction independently. The main thread waits for all child threads to finish before exiting.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of threads created
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for thread stacks plus O(1) for main program

examples.title: Mutex for Mutual Exclusion
examples.description: Using mutex to protect shared counter from race conditions in multithreaded program
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates proper use of mutex for mutual exclusion. Without mutex, the final counter would be unpredictable due to race conditions. With mutex lock/unlock around the critical section (sharedCounter++), the counter will be exactly 400000.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n×m) where n=threads, m=iterations per thread
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space plus O(n) for thread stacks

examples.title: Semaphore Implementation of Producer-Consumer
examples.description: Solution to bounded buffer problem using counting semaphores
examples.explanation: The producer waits for empty slots, adds item, signals full slots. The consumer waits for full slots, removes item, signals empty slots. The mutex ensures only one thread accesses the buffer at a time. This prevents both overflow and underflow of the bounded buffer.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for buffer where n=buffer size (10)

examples.title: Thread Pool Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates thread pool pattern for managing concurrent task execution
examples.code:
import threading
from queue import Queue

class ThreadPool:
    def __init__(self, num_threads):
        self.tasks = Queue()
        self.threads = []
        self.num_threads = num_threads
        
        # Create worker threads
        for i in range(num_threads):
            t = threading.Thread(target=self.worker)
            t.start()
            self.threads.append(t)
    
    def worker(self):
        while True:
            task = self.tasks.get()
            if task is None:  # Poison pill - shutdown signal
                break
            func, args = task
            func(*args)  # Execute the task
            self.tasks.task_done()
    
    def submit(self, func, *args):
        """Submit a task to the thread pool"""
        self.tasks.put((func, args))
    
    def shutdown(self):
        """Shutdown the thread pool"""
        # Send poison pills to stop workers
        for _ in range(self.num_threads):
            self.tasks.put(None)
        
        # Wait for all tasks to complete
        self.tasks.join()
        
        # Wait for threads to finish
        for t in self.threads:
            t.join()

# Example usage
def process_task(task_id):
    print(f"Processing task {task_id}")

pool = ThreadPool(4)  # Create pool with 4 workers

# Submit 10 tasks
for i in range(10):
    pool.submit(process_task, i)

pool.shutdown()
print("All tasks completed")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation shows a thread pool pattern. Tasks are submitted to a queue and workers pick them up for execution. This avoids the overhead of creating new threads for each task. The None poison pill signals workers to exit when shutdown is called.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for submitting n tasks, O(1) amortized for task execution
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for task queue plus O(t) for thread stacks where t=number of threads
